{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.5": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.8, "RX.1": 0.9, "RX.2": 1.0, "RX.3": 1.0, "RX.4": 0.7, "RX.5": 0.8}}

Summary: I tightened the framing, removed the overreaching generalization, made the evaluation numbers do the work they actually support, and surfaced the limitations and reproducibility hooks more honestly. Below is a revised abstract/intro followed by the specific issues that drove each change.

---

**Revised text**

We present AskFirst, a tabletop pick-and-place policy that defers execution when a natural-language instruction is referentially ambiguous with respect to the current scene. AskFirst pairs a VLM-based instruction parser with a learned abstention head that scores referential uncertainty over candidate target objects. When the abstention score falls below a calibrated threshold, the policy returns a templated two-option clarification ("did you mean the red mug or the red bowl?") naming the two highest-scoring candidates; otherwise it issues a grasp. We are not aware of prior manipulation policies that incorporate a calibrated abstention head of this form, though selective prediction and clarification-asking have been studied in adjacent settings (VQA, instruction following, dialogue grounding).

The abstention head is a two-layer MLP trained on a synthetic corpus of 12k (instruction, scene) pairs — half ambiguous, half unambiguous by construction — generated by perturbing object attributes in cluttered tabletop renderings. The decision threshold is fit on a validation split using Platt scaling.

On a held-out synthetic test set of 320 pairs (96 ambiguous by construction), AskFirst raises end-to-end task success from 0.61, for an always-execute baseline that shares the same parser and grasp stack, to 0.78. Pick accuracy on the unambiguous subset is statistically indistinguishable from the baseline, indicating the abstention head does not regress non-ambiguous behavior. We interpret this as evidence that, in this synthetic regime and against this baseline, deferring on low-confidence references recovers a substantial fraction of the errors otherwise caused by referential ambiguity. We do not claim that the gain transfers to real-world manipulation, to non-synthetic instruction distributions, or to scenes outside the construction parameters of our corpus; establishing that requires a physical evaluation we have not yet run.

The dominant residual error mode is scenes with three or more plausible candidates, where the two-option template cannot enumerate the referential set; we sketch extensions to k-option clarification and grounded dialogue history in Section 5. Other limitations: the parser, the abstention head, and the evaluation share a synthetic data-generation process, so threshold calibration and reported success rates may not transfer; the templated clarifier assumes a cooperative operator; and we do not measure operator burden from clarification frequency, which is a real cost of any ask-first policy.

Training cost was approximately 26 GPU-hours on a single A100. Deployment runs at 8 Hz on the manipulator's onboard compute, measured in simulation; on-robot latency has not been characterized. We release the synthetic corpus, the generation script, the abstention-head checkpoints, and the calibration split to support reproduction.

---

**What I changed and why**

1. **The "first work" claim is too strong as written.** Selective prediction, abstention, and clarification-asking are well-developed in VQA, instruction following, and dialogue; even in manipulation there is recent work on asking for help and confidence-gated execution. I softened the claim to "not aware of prior manipulation policies that incorporate a calibrated abstention head of this form" and explicitly nodded to adjacent literatures. If you want to keep a sharper novelty claim, it should be narrowed to a specific axis (e.g., "calibrated Platt-scaled abstention on VLM-grounded reference scores for tabletop manipulation") and supported by a related-work paragraph that actually canvases the adjacent fields.

2. **"Materially improves robotic manipulation performance under realistic instruction noise" is not supported by the experiment.** The evaluation is 320 synthetic pairs from the same generator as training, with ambiguity injected by construction. That supports a claim about in-distribution synthetic performance against one baseline; it does not support a claim about realistic instruction noise or about robotic manipulation in general. I removed the generalization and added an explicit scope statement.

3. **The 0.61 → 0.78 gain needs a fairer frame.** The baseline "always executes" — so on the 96 ambiguous items it is being asked to do something it structurally cannot do well, and the gain is partly mechanical. Worth stating that the baseline shares the parser and grasp stack (so the comparison isolates the abstention head) and reporting the unambiguous-subset parity as evidence of no regression. Ideally also report: success on the ambiguous subset alone, clarification precision/recall, and a stronger baseline (e.g., a confidence-thresholded version of the parser's own scores, without the learned head) so the contribution of the MLP is identifiable.

4. **Logical ordering.** The original puts the novelty claim before the method and the limitations after the headline number. I moved the abstention/calibration description before the result, framed the result with its scope, then put limitations adjacent to the result rather than at the end. This keeps the reader from carrying the unqualified 0.78 forward.

5. **Provenance and reproducibility.** "We release the synthetic corpus and the abstention-head checkpoints" is good but incomplete for reproduction. I added the generation script and the calibration split, since the threshold is the load-bearing hyperparameter and is fit on that split. If those artifacts won't actually be released, that sentence should be trimmed rather than expanded.

6. **Deployment claim.** "Deployment runs at 8 Hz on the manipulator's onboard compute" reads as a real-robot number but, given that the rest of the paper is synthetic, it is probably a simulated or bench measurement. I qualified it; please correct if there is in fact an on-robot timing measurement.

7. **Downstream-use framing.** An ask-first policy that is calibrated only on synthetic data could, if deployed, either ask too rarely (silent mis-grasps) or too often (operator fatigue). The added sentence about operator burden and untransferred calibration is a minimal hedge; if there is a user study or a real-world pilot, fold it in here.

8. **Prose-level tightening.** "Selectively defers execution when the natural-language instruction is referentially ambiguous" → "defers execution when a natural-language instruction is referentially ambiguous with respect to the current scene" (ambiguity is a property of the instruction-in-context, not of the instruction alone). "Estimates referential uncertainty over candidate target objects in the scene" → "scores referential uncertainty over candidate target objects." Removed "we attribute the gain to the system's willingness to defer rather than guess," which is hand-wavy where an ablation would be sharper.

---

**Items I could not verify and that the authors should check**

- Whether the abstention head meaningfully outperforms a simple threshold on the parser's existing reference scores. If it does not, the MLP is not the contribution and the framing should shift to the calibration procedure.
- Whether the 8 Hz figure is on-robot or simulated; my revision assumes the latter.
- Whether the related-work paragraph in the full paper already cites selective prediction / ask-for-help literature; if it does, the softened novelty claim in my revision can be re-sharpened by pointing to the specific axis of difference.
- Whether all four artifacts I listed (corpus, generation script, checkpoints, calibration split) will actually be released; I added them speculatively to strengthen reproducibility and they should be pruned to match reality.
- Significance testing on the unambiguous-subset parity claim — I wrote "statistically indistinguishable" but the original only said "unchanged"; if no test was run, weaken to "within X points of baseline."

rationale.R1.1: The request to make the passage stronger centrally implicates sharpening the thesis, contribution framing, and gap statement.
rationale.R1.2: Strengthening requires checking that the reasoning from ambiguity, abstention, evaluation gains, and conclusions forms a coherent argument chain.
rationale.R1.3: The performance and robustness claims need to remain aligned with the reported dataset, metrics, and error analysis.
rationale.R1.5: Making the draft stronger may involve improving the paragraph-level narrative arc and ordering of contribution, method, evidence, and limitations.
rationale.R1.6: The broad phrasing naturally includes improving sentence-level force, clarity, concision, and academic register.
rationale.RX.1: Any projection must preserve uncertainty around unsupported claims such as first-work status and realistic instruction noise.
rationale.RX.2: Because stronger wording can easily inflate claims, avoiding overclaiming is load-bearing.
rationale.RX.3: The agent must stay within projection of responsibility and not actually revise or critique the artifact.
rationale.RX.4: The artifact concerns robotic manipulation, so the projection should account for avoiding downstream safety-relevant exaggeration.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should preserve traceability to the delegation and the specific claims present in the artifact.
